title: Ontology and Pipeline Design Principles - Palantir Developer Community

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: The shared API layer of an organization composed of intuitive business concepts, connecting engineers, business users, and AIP agents to encode operational processes

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology building block representing a noun or business entity. Should map to natural-language business concepts and have a point of contact configured

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology building block representing a verb or operational decision. Combined with Object Types to form coherent sentences encoding business logic

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attributes of Object Types. Should be added incrementally to placeholder backing datasets during development

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationships between Object Types in the Ontology, connecting business entities

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data platform hosting the Ontology, pipelines, and applications

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Tool for building data pipelines in Foundry with an 'add data manually' feature useful for creating placeholder datasets during Ontology design

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Application for managing Object Types, including setting point of contact and configuring Ontology elements

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Design Principles
extractedEntities.description: Best practices for Ontology architecture: design for decision-making, use natural-language business concepts, avoid versioned names, split front-end and data engineering work, seek frequent feedback

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Data flow architecture principle: raw datasets land from source systems, get cleaned and enriched through pipelines, then back the Ontology Object Types

extractedEntities.name: Front-End Data-Engineering Split
extractedEntities.description: Design approach where front-end team builds Ontology objects/actions/applications using dummy data while data engineering team integrates real data in parallel

extractedEntities.name: Operational Decision Making
extractedEntities.description: Primary use case for the Ontology: providing relevant data for decisions, possible actions to record decisions, and logic to evaluate what decision to make

keyInsights: The Ontology is conceptualized as the API of an organization — a shared layer between engineers, business users, and AIP agents composed of intuitive business concepts
Design approach separates front-end Ontology building (using dummy data) from data engineering integration, allowing parallel development
Object Types and Actions should map to natural-language business concepts — if terms cannot express natural language sentences, the Ontology design is flawed
Ontology is not just a datastore but an API that must be designed and maintained; raw source system datasets should not be directly synced as Object Types
Pipeline Builder's 'add data manually' feature enables placeholder dataset creation for rapid Ontology prototyping before real data integration
Every Object Type should have a point of contact configured and backing datasets should have health checks and schedules

title: Deep Dive: Creating Your First Ontology - Palantir Learn

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: The heart of Foundry that provides a common vocabulary to an organization, making data easier to use through structured object types, relationships, and actions

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Fundamental Ontology building block created through Ontology Manager, representing a business entity

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationship definition between Object Types, created through Ontology Manager to model connections between business entities

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology component representing executable operations on objects, created and executed through Ontology Manager

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform where the Ontology lies at the heart, impacting how solutions should be designed

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for creating and managing Object Types, Link Types, and Action Types

extractedEntities.name: Object Explorer
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for exploring and interacting with existing Ontology objects

extractedEntities.name: Marketplace
extractedEntities.description: Foundry feature for installing bundles that provide pre-built Ontology configurations and sample data

extractedEntities.name: Data Lineage
extractedEntities.description: Foundry capability for tracing data flow through pipelines, showing how data transforms from source to Ontology backing datasets

extractedEntities.name: Fresh Air Operations
extractedEntities.description: Training example scenario used to demonstrate Ontology building concepts through Object Explorer and Ontology Manager

keyInsights: The Ontology lies at the heart of Foundry and its central position impacts how all Foundry-based solutions should be designed
Building an Ontology follows a structured workflow: explore existing Ontology, understand data lineage, then extend via Ontology Manager
The Ontology provides a common vocabulary to an organization, making data easier to use for all stakeholders
Ontology creation involves three core steps: create an Object Type, create a Link Type, then create and execute an Action Type
Marketplace bundles provide pre-built Ontology configurations that accelerate learning and prototyping

title: Object and link types - Properties - Mandatory control properties - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Mandatory Control Property
extractedEntities.description: Special Object Type property that enforces granular access control on data stored in objects, restricting access to all other properties in the same datasource based on markings, organizations, or classifications

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attributes of Object Types that can be configured with mandatory control settings to restrict data access

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity whose properties can be secured via mandatory control properties

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Mandatory access controls that restrict data access by requiring users to possess specific markings. Multiple markings require ALL to be satisfied (AND logic)

extractedEntities.name: Organizations
extractedEntities.description: Access requirements enforcing strict silos between groups of users. Users must be a member of at least one applied organization (OR logic)

extractedEntities.name: Classifications
extractedEntities.description: Hierarchical mandatory controls for protecting sensitive government information, defining sensitivity levels in a hierarchical way

extractedEntities.name: Restricted View
extractedEntities.description: Marking-backed security layer that must be created before mandatory control properties can be configured on an Object Type

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V2
extractedEntities.description: Required storage version for mandatory control properties; these controls are not available on Object Storage V1

extractedEntities.name: CBAC
extractedEntities.description: Classification-Based Access Control - when enabled, provides option for classification-based mandatory controls on properties

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Application where mandatory control properties are configured on Object Types

keyInsights: Mandatory control properties enforce access control at the property level within an Object Type, restricting access to all properties in the same datasource
Three types of mandatory controls exist: Markings (AND logic - user needs ALL), Organizations (OR logic - user needs at least ONE), and Classifications (hierarchical sensitivity levels)
Mandatory control properties require Object Storage V2 and are not available on V1 (Phonograph)
A marking-backed restricted view must be created before configuring mandatory control properties
Markings and organizations can be combined on the same property: user must satisfy ALL markings AND at least ONE organization
For multi-datasource Object Types, each datasource needs its own mandatory control property configuration

title: Object permissioning - Multi-datasource object types (MDOs) - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Multi-Datasource Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Object Type backed by multiple datasources, enabling column-level access controls where distinct property subsets come from different datasources joined by primary key

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity that can be backed by single or multiple datasources

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attributes of Object Types; in MDOs, a specific property must come from exactly one datasource except for the primary key

extractedEntities.name: Primary Key
extractedEntities.description: Required unique identifier property that must exist in every backing datasource of an MDO to enable joining

extractedEntities.name: Backing Datasource
extractedEntities.description: A dataset or restricted view that provides data for an Object Type. MDOs have multiple backing datasources

extractedEntities.name: Column-Wise MDO
extractedEntities.description: Join-like MDO where distinct subsets of properties are integrated from different datasources, supporting column-level access controls

extractedEntities.name: Row-Wise MDO
extractedEntities.description: Union-like MDO concept where full objects come from multiple datasources sharing the same schema. Not supported in Foundry; restricted views cover most use cases

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V2
extractedEntities.description: Required storage version for MDOs; not available on Object Storage V1 (Phonograph)

extractedEntities.name: Restricted View
extractedEntities.description: Alternative to row-wise MDOs for row-level access controls; can be used as backing datasources for MDOs

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Application where MDOs are configured by adding multiple backing datasources and mapping primary keys

extractedEntities.name: Column-Level Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Security pattern using column-wise MDOs to restrict property visibility based on datasource permissions

keyInsights: MDOs only support column-wise joins (not row-wise unions); row-wise use cases should use restricted views instead
Each property in an MDO must come from exactly one datasource, but the primary key must exist in every datasource for joining
If a user lacks Viewer permission on a backing datasource, properties from that datasource appear as null but the object type schema remains visible
MDOs require Object Storage V2 and support user edits and materializations
Only Foundry datasets or restricted views can serve as MDO backing datasources; streaming sources are not supported
Property multiplicity (overlapping columns from multiple datasources) is not supported in column-wise MDOs

title: Action types - Action log - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Action Log
extractedEntities.description: Ontology feature that models all action submissions as object types for audit and analysis, capturing decisions made through actions and making them available as data in the Ontology

extractedEntities.name: Action Log Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Automatically generated Object Type prefaced with [LOG] that maps one-to-one with an Action Type, storing submission metadata and automatically linking to edited objects

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology component representing executable operations. When configured with an action log, each submission generates a single log object

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity that can be modified by Action Types and tracked via Action Log

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Foreign key links automatically created between Action Log objects and all objects edited by the submitted action

extractedEntities.name: Action Log Schema
extractedEntities.description: Default schema storing Action RID, Action Type RID, version, UTC timestamp, userId, edited objects primary keys, optional summary and parameter values

extractedEntities.name: Action RID
extractedEntities.description: Unique identifier for a single action submission stored in the action log

extractedEntities.name: Edit History
extractedEntities.description: Alternative to action log for use cases where logging all edits to an object is desired, enabled per Object Type

extractedEntities.name: Decision Audit Trail
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of using action logs to capture operational decisions and their context, enabling compliance and decision analysis workflows

extractedEntities.name: Close Alerts Audit
extractedEntities.description: Example use case where a Close Alerts action closing 10 Alert objects yields a single action log object linked to all 10 edited objects

keyInsights: Action log object types map one-to-one with action types — each submission generates exactly one log object regardless of how many objects are edited
Action logs capture not just edits but the broader decision context: concurrent edits, world state at submission time, and configurable property snapshots
All action log object types are automatically prefaced with [LOG] for easy identification in the Ontology
Action logs support storing unedited properties of referenced objects to capture contextual information beyond the actual modifications
For pure edit tracking without decision context, edit history should be used instead of action logs
Users need appropriate permissions on the action log object type to apply action log-backed action types

title: GitHub - palantir/foundry-platform-python: The official Python SDK for the Foundry API

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Platform SDK (Python)
extractedEntities.description: Official Python SDK auto-generated from the Foundry API specification for interacting with the Foundry platform. Available on PyPI as foundry-platform-sdk

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Ontology SDK for interacting directly with Foundry ontologies — recommended over the Platform SDK for applications leveraging the Ontology

extractedEntities.name: Gotham Platform SDK
extractedEntities.description: SDK generated from the Gotham API specification for interacting with the Gotham platform including Gaia, Target Workbench, and geotemporal data

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: Portal for creating and managing applications using Palantir APIs, used to generate the Ontology SDK

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data platform exposing versioned API endpoints for programmatic interaction

extractedEntities.name: FoundryClient
extractedEntities.description: Main Python client class for the Foundry Platform SDK v2, located in the foundry_sdk package

extractedEntities.name: API Versioning
extractedEntities.description: Foundry API uses URL-based versioning (v1, v2) with separate client classes per major version

extractedEntities.name: OAuth2 Client Credentials
extractedEntities.description: Authentication flow supported by the Python SDK for machine-to-machine access to the Foundry API

extractedEntities.name: User Token Authentication
extractedEntities.description: Authentication method using user-issued tokens for accessing the Foundry API through the SDK

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontological resource accessible through the Foundry Platform SDK at the type/metadata level

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontological resource accessible through the Foundry Platform SDK at the type/metadata level

keyInsights: Palantir provides three distinct SDKs: Foundry Platform SDK (platform operations), OSDK (ontology interaction), and Gotham Platform SDK (Gotham-specific) — choosing the correct one is critical
The Foundry Platform SDK surfaces endpoints for Ontological resources at the type/metadata level, while OSDK allows direct interaction with objects, links, and actions
Applications leveraging the Ontology should prefer OSDK over the Foundry Platform SDK for a superior development experience
The Python SDK is auto-generated from the Foundry API specification and supports both user tokens and OAuth2 client credentials
API versioning uses URL paths (e.g., /api/v1/, /api/v2/) with separate client classes per major version

title: Object and link types - Properties - Metadata reference - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Ontology element with rich metadata including ID, display name, description, RID, status, API name, keys, base type, value formatting, conditional formatting, type classes, and render hints

extractedEntities.name: Property ID
extractedEntities.description: Unique identifier for a property, used to reference it when configuring applications (e.g., 'start-date')

extractedEntities.name: Property API Name
extractedEntities.description: Name used when referring to the property programmatically in code (e.g., 'startDate')

extractedEntities.name: Property RID
extractedEntities.description: Automatically generated unique identifier for every resource in Foundry, referenced in error messages across the platform

extractedEntities.name: Property Base Type
extractedEntities.description: Indicates the type of values for a property, determining available operations in user applications (e.g., date, string, numeric)

extractedEntities.name: Primary Key
extractedEntities.description: Property acting as unique identifier for each object instance — each row in backing datasources must have a different value

extractedEntities.name: Title Key
extractedEntities.description: Property that acts as a display name for objects of a type, shown to users in applications

extractedEntities.name: Render Hints
extractedEntities.description: Indications to user applications about how to render a property differently than default for its base type

extractedEntities.name: Type Classes
extractedEntities.description: Additional metadata on properties interpreted by user applications for special handling

extractedEntities.name: Conditional Formatting
extractedEntities.description: Rules on a property that dictate how its value renders (coloring, alignment, etc.) based on conditions in user-facing applications

extractedEntities.name: Property Status
extractedEntities.description: Development lifecycle signal: active, experimental, or deprecated. Default is experimental

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity that contains properties with rich metadata configurations

keyInsights: Properties have dual identity: a human-readable display name and a programmatic API name used in code
Every Foundry resource gets an automatically generated RID used in error messages across the platform
Properties follow a lifecycle status model (experimental by default, then active or deprecated) signaling development readiness
The primary key property enforces uniqueness across all backing datasource rows, while the title key provides human-readable display names
Value formatting, conditional formatting, and render hints give fine-grained control over how property values appear in user applications
Type classes provide extensible metadata interpreted by user applications for specialized behavior beyond base type defaults

title: Object and link types - Link types - Metadata reference - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology element defining relationships between Object Types with bidirectional display names, cardinality constraints, and key mappings

extractedEntities.name: Link Type ID
extractedEntities.description: Unique identifier for a link type used when configuring applications (e.g., 'employee-employer')

extractedEntities.name: Link Type API Name
extractedEntities.description: Name used when referring to the link type programmatically in code

extractedEntities.name: Link Type Cardinality
extractedEntities.description: Constraint indicating if each side has one or many objects: one-to-one, one-to-many, or many-to-many

extractedEntities.name: Foreign Key
extractedEntities.description: Property of one object type referencing the primary key of another, used in one-to-one and one-to-many link types

extractedEntities.name: Join Table
extractedEntities.description: Table containing pairs of primary keys defining links in many-to-many cardinality link types

extractedEntities.name: Link Type Display Name
extractedEntities.description: Bidirectional display names — each side of a link type has its own display name and optional plural display name

extractedEntities.name: Link Type RID
extractedEntities.description: Automatically generated unique identifier referenced in error messages across the platform

extractedEntities.name: Link Type Status
extractedEntities.description: Development lifecycle signal: active, experimental, or deprecated. Default is experimental

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: The two sides of a link type, each representing a business entity connected by the relationship

keyInsights: Link types are bidirectional: each side has its own display name and optional plural display name to describe the relationship from each direction
Three cardinality patterns exist: one-to-one (foreign key), one-to-many (foreign key), and many-to-many (join table with paired primary keys)
Many-to-many link types require a dedicated join table containing pairs of primary keys, whereas one-to-one/one-to-many use foreign key references
Like properties, link types follow a lifecycle status model (experimental, active, deprecated) and have both human-readable and programmatic API names
Link type metadata includes a unique ID for application configuration and an auto-generated RID for platform-wide referencing

title: What is a data pipeline? - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Data Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Flow of data from a source system through intermediate datasets to produce high-quality curated datasets that can be structured into the Ontology or serve ML/analytical workflows

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool enabling users to quickly set up pipelines with a point-and-click interface, supporting both technical and less-technical users

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Data platform providing the digital view of objective reality within an organization through data integration

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity backed by curated datasets produced through data pipelines

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Ownership
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where each production pipeline has designated person(s) overseeing regular, reliable data flow to power business processes

extractedEntities.name: Batch Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline type for periodic data processing at scheduled intervals

extractedEntities.name: Incremental Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline type for processing only new or changed data since the last run

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline type for continuous real-time data processing

extractedEntities.name: Build Scheduling
extractedEntities.description: Configuration of recurring data transformation runs, from weekly/daily to hourly or more frequent cadences

extractedEntities.name: Data Expectations
extractedEntities.description: Quality checks on pipeline inputs and outputs at every step, recommended during initial pipeline definition stages

keyInsights: Data pipelines serve to provide a digital view of objective reality by syncing data from source systems, imposing common schema, and combining datasets
Three pipeline types available in Foundry: batch, incremental, and streaming — choice depends on data scale, latency requirements, and maintenance complexity
A production-quality pipeline requires ownership, build scheduling, data quality checks, security governance, and documentation
Pipeline Builder enables both technical users (rapid pipeline building) and less-technical users (point-and-click forms) to create pipelines
Curated datasets produced by pipelines can either be structured into the Ontology or serve as foundation for ML and analytical workflows

title: Pipeline Builder - Core Concepts - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool for building data pipelines with graph and form-based interfaces, supporting datasets, virtual tables, and Ontology components as outputs

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: Building block of a pipeline — a wrapper around a collection of files stored in a backing file system, representing data from landing to Ontology mapping

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Branch
extractedEntities.description: Version control mechanism in Pipeline Builder operating like Git branches, allowing isolated iteration without affecting the main pipeline

extractedEntities.name: Transform
extractedEntities.description: Function definition accepting inputs (datasets) and producing outputs, forming the links between datasets in a pipeline

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Output
extractedEntities.description: Result of transforms: can be datasets, virtual tables, or Ontology components (object types, link types, time series)

extractedEntities.name: Build Schedule
extractedEntities.description: Recurring build configuration that can trigger at specific times, cadences, or based on upstream resource status updates

extractedEntities.name: Data Expectations
extractedEntities.description: Quality checks on outputs and intermediate transforms supporting primary key and row count expectation types

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology component that can be produced as a Pipeline Builder output

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology component that can be produced as a Pipeline Builder output

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform providing the dataset, branching, and version control infrastructure used by Pipeline Builder

keyInsights: Pipeline Builder uses Git-like branching for version control: users iterate on branches and propose merges back to Main, just like code pull requests
Pipeline outputs are not limited to datasets — they can be virtual tables or Ontology components including object types, link types, and time series
Transforms are function definitions accepting inputs and producing outputs, forming the logical links in a pipeline graph
Data expectations currently support two types: primary key validation and row count checks on pipeline outputs
Build schedules can be time-based, cadence-based, or event-driven (triggered by upstream resource updates)

title: Pipeline Builder - Transforms - Overview - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry data transformation tool providing a general model for describing transformations with an intermediate layer between writing tools and execution engines

extractedEntities.name: Expression
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline Builder data transformation that takes columns as input and outputs a single column (e.g., Split string, Cast, Concatenate strings, Length)

extractedEntities.name: Transform
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline Builder data transformation that takes an entire table as input and returns an entire table (e.g., Pivot, Filter, Drop columns)

extractedEntities.name: Join Transform
extractedEntities.description: Combines two datasets with at least one matching column, with configurable join types for matching/excluding rows

extractedEntities.name: Union Transform
extractedEntities.description: Combines two datasets to include all rows, requiring all inputs to have the same schema

extractedEntities.name: User-Defined Function
extractedEntities.description: Custom code (Python recommended, Java available) that can be versioned and upgraded, for cases where built-in transforms are insufficient

extractedEntities.name: No-Code Data Transformation
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline Builder's approach of abstracting programming languages behind a streamlined builder interface for data transformation

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology component that can be produced as output from Pipeline Builder transforms

keyInsights: Pipeline Builder uses a general intermediate layer between transformation writing and execution, supporting multiple output types including datasets, Ontology objects, streams, and time-series
Two transformation categories exist: expressions (column-in, column-out) and transforms (table-in, table-out), unified in the same configuration interface
Expressions can be embedded within transforms (e.g., Length expression inside a Filter transform) for composable data manipulation
User-defined functions (Python recommended) provide escape hatch for complex logic but should be used sparingly in favor of optimized built-in transforms
Pipeline Builder decouples transformation logic from specific execution engines, allowing the same transformation model to target batch, streaming, or Ontology outputs

title: Data Connection - Core Concepts - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for connecting to external systems, managing sources, credentials, workers, and syncs

extractedEntities.name: Source
extractedEntities.description: A single connection to an external system including configuration to specify/locate the target and credentials for authentication

extractedEntities.name: Connector
extractedEntities.description: Source type defining the kind of external system to connect to (e.g., Postgres, S3, REST API). Also called source type

extractedEntities.name: Sync
extractedEntities.description: Configuration for bringing specific sets of data from a source into Foundry as datasets

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Worker
extractedEntities.description: Default compute worker running source capabilities in Foundry's containerized, scalable job execution environment

extractedEntities.name: Agent Worker
extractedEntities.description: Advanced compute worker running on customer-provided host via agent, for specific file-based syncs or micro-batching with on-premise systems

extractedEntities.name: Credential
extractedEntities.description: Encrypted secret value (password, token, API key) required to authenticate with external systems, stored securely in the platform

extractedEntities.name: Egress Policy
extractedEntities.description: Network policy controlling outbound connections from Foundry worker sources to external systems

extractedEntities.name: External Transform
extractedEntities.description: Code-based connectivity option for systems without dedicated connectors, using the generic or REST API source

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform hosting Data Connection and providing the compute infrastructure for data syncing

keyInsights: Data Connection supports two compute models: Foundry worker (preferred, containerized, scalable) and Agent worker (for on-premise systems not accessible over the Internet)
Sources are configured based on connectors (source types) with a broad range available for common enterprise data systems
For systems without dedicated connectors, generic connector or REST API source can be used with code-based options like external transforms and compute modules
All credentials are encrypted and stored securely, with some sources supporting secretless authentication via OpenID Connect or cloud identity
Foundry worker sources benefit from containerized, scalable job execution and now support both direct egress and agent proxy networking

title: Data Connection - Sources - Set up a source - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application where sources are created and configured to connect external systems to Foundry

extractedEntities.name: Source Setup
extractedEntities.description: Four-step process: validate network connection, provision credentials, configure agent drivers (if agent worker), create source in Data Connection UI

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Worker Source
extractedEntities.description: Connection type running compute in Foundry, supporting direct egress and agent proxy networking

extractedEntities.name: Agent Worker Source
extractedEntities.description: Connection type running compute on customer-provided host via agent, requiring two network paths: agent-to-external-system and agent-to-Foundry

extractedEntities.name: Agent
extractedEntities.description: Single point of validated entry from customer network to Foundry, handling data reading and sending with traffic flowing only outbound from agent

extractedEntities.name: Service Account
extractedEntities.description: Dedicated credentials provisioned specifically for Foundry source authentication, separate from personal accounts

extractedEntities.name: Egress Policy
extractedEntities.description: Network policy for Foundry worker sources enabling direct connection or agent proxy connections

extractedEntities.name: Sync
extractedEntities.description: Configuration for bringing specific data into Foundry from a connected source, entirely configurable through Data Connection UI

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform receiving data from external systems through configured sources

keyInsights: Source setup requires network validation, credential provisioning, optional agent configuration, and UI-based source creation — syncs are then entirely configurable through Data Connection
Agent worker networking requires two separate paths: agent-to-external-system and agent-to-Foundry, with traffic only flowing outbound from the agent
Dedicated service accounts should be provisioned for Foundry authentication rather than using personal credentials
Foundry worker sources now support both direct egress policies and agent proxy policies, making 'direct connection' naming obsolete
Source types include Postgres databases, S3 buckets, Linux filesystems, SAP instances, REST APIs, and many more enterprise data systems

title: Core Concepts - Branching - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Dataset Branch
extractedEntities.description: Version control mechanism for datasets analogous to Git branches — a pointer to the most recent transaction, enabling concurrent work without interference

extractedEntities.name: Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Unit of dataset change analogous to a Git commit, forming a linear sequence on a branch ordered by commit time

extractedEntities.name: Commit
extractedEntities.description: Record of changes (additions and removals) made within a branch, tracked for clear history

extractedEntities.name: Pull Request
extractedEntities.description: Signal to team that branch changes are ready for review and merging back to master, enabling code review workflow

extractedEntities.name: Data Version Control
extractedEntities.description: Pattern applying software version control best practices to data pipelines: branch, commit, pull request, review, merge

extractedEntities.name: Logic and Data Branching
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's combination of Git-based code branching with dataset branching for simultaneous versioning of both logic and data

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform implementing dataset branching alongside Git-like version control for code, enabling branching over logic and data simultaneously

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool supporting branching workflow for data pipeline development

extractedEntities.name: Code Repositories
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application implementing Git-like version control for data transformation code, designed for one active developer per branch

keyInsights: Foundry uniquely combines dataset branching with Git-based code branching, enabling simultaneous versioning of both logic and data
Dataset branches are analogous to Git branches: datasets, branches, and transactions map to Git repositories, branches, and commits respectively
The branching workflow follows standard Git patterns: create branch, make commits, create pull request, review, merge to master
Code Repositories is designed for one active developer per branch — concurrent edits on the same branch risk overwrites
Branching enables safe experimentation: users work in isolation and merge only validated changes back to the production pipeline

title: Why Data Pipeline Version Control Matters - Palantir Blog

extractedEntities.name: Data Pipeline Version Control
extractedEntities.description: Practice of tracking changes to both code (transformation logic) and data together, enabling safe scheduling, auditability, and understanding which code+data combination produced which outcome

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform launched in 2016 with heavy focus on data pipeline tooling and version control as a key component

extractedEntities.name: Gotham
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's earlier platform featuring the Revisioning Database, a framework applying version control concepts to graph databases

extractedEntities.name: Revisioning Database
extractedEntities.description: One of Palantir's first technologies applying version control concepts to graph databases in the Gotham platform

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: Collection of data files (spreadsheets, documents, images, etc.) that form the nodes of a data pipeline

extractedEntities.name: Transform
extractedEntities.description: Software code/logic that changes a dataset — a pipeline can have hundreds of transforms applying incremental changes

extractedEntities.name: Metadata Tracking
extractedEntities.description: Tracking source system origin, security policies, developer changes alongside data, often more important than tracking data changes themselves

extractedEntities.name: Role-Based Data Views
extractedEntities.description: Version control enables users with different roles to see different versions of the same data file based on their respective authorizations

extractedEntities.name: Versioned Output Dataset
extractedEntities.description: Dataset with version history enabling safe scheduling of transformation jobs across the platform without risk of data corruption

keyInsights: Version control for data pipelines tracks both code changes (transformation logic) and data changes simultaneously, with metadata often more important than the data changes themselves
Palantir's version control obsession began with the Revisioning Database in Gotham, applying VC concepts to graph databases before extending to Foundry
Metadata captured includes source system origin, security policies, and developer attribution — informing much of the end-user experience including role-based data visibility
Versioned output datasets enable safe scheduling of transformation jobs across the platform without risk of data corruption from concurrent writes
Understanding which combination of code and data produced which outcome is the key value proposition of data pipeline version control
